title: Blood on White Cotton — The Classic Test of a Good Spotter
stainChemistry: Blood on white cotton is dominated by hemoglobin — a protein carrying an iron-porphyrin (heme) group. The protein portion hydrogen-bonds to cotton's cellulose hydroxyl groups, while the iron oxidizes from Fe²⁺ (red) to Fe³⁺ (brown) over time. Fresh blood is water-soluble; dried blood cross-links through both protein coagulation and iron oxidation. White cotton is the most forgiving surface for blood — it tolerates the full chemistry arsenal including enzymes, peroxide, and even dilute chlorine bleach for stubborn iron shadows.
whyThisWorks: White cotton tolerates protease enzymes (cotton is cellulose, not protein), full-strength protein formula, hydrogen peroxide, and even controlled bleaching. This makes it the most treatable combination. Dan Eisen's two-method escalation works perfectly here: Method 1 (enzyme detergent in warm water) handles fresh blood. Method 2 (detergent → ammonia → peroxide+ammonia → vinegar) handles set blood. The iron shadow responds to peroxide's oxidative bleaching, and on white cotton there's no dye to worry about damaging.

spottingProtocol.technique: Flush from back of fabric using water gun
spottingProtocol.temperature: Cold — below 100°F per Dan Eisen. Cold water dissolves fresh hemoglobin; hot water cooks it permanently.
spottingProtocol.instruction: Place cotton face-down on towel over vacuum. Flush with cold water gun from back. On cotton you can use moderate pressure — cotton is strong wet. Watch towel for red/brown transfer. Most fresh blood comes out with cold water alone. This is your golden window.
Apply NSD to remaining stain. Cotton tolerates brush action well — use light tamping with spotting brush to work lubricant into fabric. The NSD suspends loosened protein for easier flushing.

spottingProtocol.agent: Protein formula (full strength) OR enzyme digester
spottingProtocol.temperature: Warm (100–110°F) for enzyme — this activates the protease. Cool for chemical protein formula.
spottingProtocol.dwellTime: 5–15 minutes for enzyme digester; 3–5 minutes for chemical protein formula
spottingProtocol.instruction: On white cotton, you have two excellent options: (A) Enzyme digester with protease — warm water activates the enzyme. Protease cleaves hemoglobin's peptide bonds into water-soluble fragments. This is safe on cotton because cotton is cellulose, not protein. Dwell 10–15 minutes for set blood. (B) Street's Protein Formula — chemical approach using mild alkali. Apply full strength, tamp, dwell 3–5 minutes. Choose enzyme for set/dried blood, chemical for fresh. Tamp periodically to bring fresh chemistry to the stain.
Flush all chemistry through. On cotton, you can be thorough — cotton handles water well. Inspect: if stain is gone, proceed to final rinse. If a brown shadow remains, continue to step 5.

spottingProtocol.temperature: Warm — you can now use gentle steam on cotton since protein was removed in steps 1–4
spottingProtocol.instruction: For residual brown iron shadow: apply 6% hydrogen peroxide, add 1 drop 28% ammonia per tablespoon. On white cotton, this is safe and effective — the peroxide oxidizes the Fe³⁺ chromophore. You may see fizzing (catalase reaction with residual blood cells). Steam chase is now safe since protein has been removed. Watch the brown fade to clear. If stain lightens but doesn't fully clear, a second application usually finishes it.
Apply dilute acetic acid (1:10) to neutralize residual ammonia. On cotton this prevents residual alkaline chemistry from weakening cellulose over time. Flush thoroughly with cold water.

spottingProtocol.technique: Complete water flush, machine dry safe
spottingProtocol.instruction: Final cold water flush. White cotton can be machine dried ONLY after confirming the stain is completely gone. If any shadow remains, air dry and repeat — heat from the dryer will permanently set any residual iron staining.

homeSolutions: FRESH: Run cold water through the back of the fabric immediately — hold under the faucet with the stain facing down so water pushes blood OUT of the fabric, not through it. Cold water alone removes most fresh blood on cotton. Follow with a cold soak in enzyme detergent (Tide Original, not Free & Clear — you WANT the enzymes here) for 30 minutes.

SET/DRIED: Soak in cold water with enzyme detergent (1 part Tide to 5 parts cold water, per Dan Eisen) for at least 30 minutes. Then apply 3% hydrogen peroxide directly to the brown shadow — you'll see fizzing as it reacts with the blood. Blot, repeat. Follow with a cold wash cycle with Tide.

NUCLEAR OPTION FOR WHITE COTTON ONLY: If a faint shadow persists after enzyme + peroxide, you can carefully use diluted chlorine bleach (1:10 Clorox to water, or use Clorox Bleach Pen for precision). Apply for 2–3 minutes, rinse thoroughly. This is ONLY safe on white cotton — never on colors, silk, wool, or synthetics. Dan Eisen recommends Clorox Bleach Stick as a final step for stubborn protein stains on whites.

WHEN TO STOP: If the stain doesn't respond to enzyme soak + peroxide + bleach, it has likely undergone thermal coagulation (was heated at some point) and is chemically bonded to the fiber. Take it to a professional — they have stronger chemistry and equipment.
materialWarnings: NEVER hot water as the first step — coagulates hemoglobin permanently. Dan Eisen: 'First wash must not exceed 100°F.'
NEVER use the dryer until the stain is confirmed gone — dryer heat (120°F+) permanently sets any residual blood.
NEVER alcohol on blood — per Dan Eisen, alcohol makes protein stains 'virtually impossible to remove, even by the best drycleaners.'
NEVER apply chlorine bleach on colored cotton — strips dye permanently. Chlorine bleach is ONLY for white cotton as a last resort.

professional.name: Street's Protein Formula
professional.use: Primary chemical protein treatment — use full strength on cotton
professional.note: Alkaline protein spotter. Safe at full strength on cotton. Tamp and flush after 3–5 minutes.

professional.name: Prochem Power Burst
professional.use: Enzyme pre-spray for protein stain digestion — ideal for set blood on cotton
professional.note: Protease + amylase in alkaline builder. Dilute per label, apply warm (100–110°F), dwell 15–20 minutes. Cotton's cellulose fiber is immune to protease — enzymes attack only the blood protein.

professional.name: Hydrogen Peroxide 6%
professional.use: Residual brown iron shadow removal after protein treatment
professional.note: With ammonia catalyst. Safe on white cotton at full professional strength. Steam-chase accelerates the reaction.

professional.name: Bridgepoint Stain Zone
professional.use: Heavy-duty oxidizer for stubborn iron staining on white cotton
professional.note: Stabilized peroxide system. Heat-activate with steam. Use when standard 6% peroxide leaves a shadow.

consumer.name: Tide Original Liquid
consumer.use: Enzyme detergent soak — protease digests blood protein
consumer.note: Mix 1 part Tide to 5 parts cold water. Soak 30 minutes minimum. The enzymes need time to work — don't rush this step.

consumer.name: Hydrogen Peroxide 3%
consumer.use: Drugstore peroxide for brown iron shadow on white cotton
consumer.note: Apply directly, let fizz, blot. Repeat 2–3 times. Safe on white cotton. Test on colors first — may lighten dyes.

consumer.name: Clorox Bleach Pen
consumer.use: Precision chlorine bleach for final stubborn shadow on WHITE cotton only
consumer.note: Dan Eisen's recommended final step for persistent protein stains on whites. Apply, wait 2–3 minutes, rinse thoroughly. NEVER on colors, silk, wool, or synthetics.

escalation.when: Blood stain persists after enzyme soak + peroxide + bleach attempt, or the garment was machine-dried before treatment (thermal coagulation), or the stain is very old (weeks+) and has fully oxidized.
escalation.whatToTell: Tell the cleaner: 'This is blood on white cotton. I treated with cold water and enzyme detergent, then hydrogen peroxide. A brown shadow remains — likely oxidized iron from the hemoglobin. The garment [was / was not] exposed to heat before I treated it.'
escalation.specialistType: Any competent dry cleaner with a spotting board should handle this. White cotton is the most treatable combination. If they can't remove blood from white cotton, find a different cleaner.

solventNote: For dry cleaners: Blood on white cotton is a wet-side stain — pre-spot with cold protein formula and flush BEFORE loading into the machine. PERC at 140°F will permanently set unspotted blood. After protein pre-spot, the garment can go through normal PERC or HC cycle. Post-clean, check for residual shadow — if present, post-spot with peroxide + ammonia on the spotting board, steam-chase. White cotton tolerates the full chemistry arsenal, so there's no excuse for returning blood-stained white cotton to a customer.
